title: IDOR — User Data
description:
A profile endpoint returns user data for any `user_id` passed in the URL without verifying that the requester owns that account. An attacker logged in as user 1 can simply change the URL to `/profile/2` to see another user's data.

Fix `get_profile()` to enforce that users can only access their own profile.
hint: Compare `user_id` from the URL against the authenticated user's ID from the session/token. Return 403 if they don't match.
explanation: Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) occur when an application uses user-controlled input to access objects without verifying authorization. Always check that the authenticated user is authorized to access the specific resource being requested.

attack_flow.label: No auth check
attack_flow.value: get_profile() returns users[2] without comparing requester vs target

attack_flow.label: Result
attack_flow.value: User 2's SSN (987-65-4321) and email exposed to user 1

why_blocked: Adding `if requesting_user_id != target_user_id: return None, 'Forbidden'` ensures each user can only fetch their own record. The ID comparison happens server-side using the authenticated identity — the client cannot forge it.
